name: Restore The Accepted Compact PTX Anchor After The Failed Existing X32 Probe

summary.name: Restore The Accepted Compact PTX Anchor After The Failed Existing X32 Probe
summary.hypothesis: The current hot-band dispatch is sitting on a clearly regressed x32 staged branch. Restoring the accepted compact PTX microkernel should immediately recover occupancy, shared-memory footprint, and the known-good branch surface before the search spends another round on new ideas.
summary.expected_bottleneck: The immediate bottleneck is not an unresolved compact-surface seam; it is the residency and sync damage introduced by the x32 staged probe.
summary.risk: Low. This is a bounded recovery back to the best-known refactor-path anchor rather than a fresh algorithmic change.
ranking_notes: This is the highest-value move because the regression is large, causal, and localized to the hot-band dispatch choice.
The accepted compact anchor is already measured on this branch, so recovery is more valuable than searching inside the broken x32 branch.
Frontier should prefer this over older restore candidates because it is tied to the latest live regression and carries the strongest current evidence.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if restoring the compact anchor requires changes beyond the fixed hot-band dispatch and its directly paired kernel selection.
expected_local_changes: Switch the fixed hot-band dispatch back from the existing 128x128x32 staged kernel to the compact 128x128 PTX microkernel.
Leave grouped_rows=4, the peeled hot-band row band, and the 64x96 tail path unchanged.
Do not bundle the restore with another sync or tiling experiment in the same round.

notes: Build passed. Node A must measure this implementation next.
